retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Community water fluoridation has been used for over half a century, and results show that the greatest effect of fluoridated water on tooth protection is in deciduous and mixed dentition. [13] [14] Water fluoridation is a simple, cost-effective method to prevent caries, and it is less than the cost of 1 dental restoration per person in their lifetime. [15] The United States Public Health Service recommends a fluoride concentration of 0.7 mg/L of water to prevent caries while reducing the risk of dental fluorosis. [16] Recently, there has been a rise in the consumption of bottled water that usually contains an insufficient concentration of fluoride. [17] In that case, alternative sources of fluoride supplementation should be considered. Also, over the decades, the protective benefits of water fluoridation have declined as other forms of fluoride delivery spread. For example, fluoride supplements are prescribed for children whose primary source of drinking water is deficient in fluoride. [13] [18] It is highly recommended by the United States Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) that primary care providers (PCPs) prescribe fluoride supplements starting at 6 months of age to infants whose primary water source is lacking in fluoride. [18] Fluoride supplements include the systemic ingestion of fluoride in the form of dietary tablets, lozenges, or drops.

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In the early years of childhood, the swallowing reflex of children is not well controlled. For this reason, toothpaste containing 1,500 ppm fluoride is contraindicated in children under 6 to minimize the risk of fluorosis due to swallowing. [19] The recommended amount of toothpaste for toothbrushing varies by age: for children under 3 years, it's a smear, and for those aged 3 to 6, it's a pea-sized amount. [27] The maximum dose of fluoride toothpaste available over the counter in most countries is 1,500 ppm. Higher amounts (2,800 ppm and 5,000 ppm) are available on prescription. However, the indication of higher doses should be implemented as a short-term intervention to reduce risk in vulnerable young people and adults while other measures are being taken, like changing a cariogenic diet. On the other hand, patients at high chronic risk of dental caries could benefit from using such high doses for the long term, eg, those with xerostomia or physical or learning disabilities. Sodium fluoride toothpaste at concentrations of 2,800 ppm can be indicated from 10 years of age, and 5,000 ppm from 16 years of age.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Fluorosis -- Treatment Planning
retrieved_docs.content: Prevention must be instigated from birth and lies primarily in the education of caregivers and government strategy and policy. These strategies include the control of fluoride levels in drinking water and educating caregivers about the etiology of fluorosis and the importance of receiving the optimum amount of fluoride. [3] This may include avoiding fluoride supplements, especially in areas with fluoridated water, and focusing on the use of topical fluorides such as those in fluoridated toothpaste and mouthwashes. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Differentiate various ways of using fluoride as a method of dental caries prevention. Assess the benefits and drawbacks of the various methods of dental caries prevention. Identify the barriers to dental caries prevention on an individual and community level. Explain the importance of collaborating with pediatric care providers to prevent early childhood caries. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride toothpaste delivers fluoride to dental surfaces, and it presents mainly as sodium fluoride (NaF). It has been effective in caries prevention of both primary and permanent teeth for decades. [21] [22] Brushing with fluoride toothpaste increases the fluoride concentration in saliva by 100- to 1,000-fold. After 1 to 2 hours of using fluoride toothpaste, the saliva concentration returns to the baseline level. [23] Toothbrushing must start when the first tooth erupts, usually as early as 6 months. [24] Brushing twice a day with fluoride toothpaste lowers the risk of dental caries by 14%. [25] Efficient plaque removal is attainable only while toothbrushing is supervised in children under 4. [26] Fluoride toothpaste containing 1,000 ppm of fluoride prevents dental caries in the permanent and primary dentition. [7] In the United States, the standard concentration of fluoride in toothpaste is 1,000 to 1,100 ppm. [19] Doses of 1,350 ppm to 1,500 ppm are advised for children at high risk of developing caries and 7 years of age upwards.

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Dietary fluoride supplements may be tablets, lozenges, or liquids such as fluoride-vitamin preparations. [19] Tablets and lozenges contain 1.0, 0.5, or 0.25 mg fluoride concentrations, and sodium fluoride is usually the active ingredient. [19] For infants, liquid fluoride supplements are used with a dropper. For children and adults, tablets are prescribed by dentists and PCPs, with instructions to chew or suck for 1 to 2 minutes before they are swallowed. This is to maximize the topical effect of fluoride. [20] One study showed an 80% reduction of caries in children who dissolved fluoride in the mouth by sucking or chewing than those who swallowed the fluoride tablets. Fluoride supplements have been prescribed to compensate for fluoride-deficient water intake. Therefore, careful consideration must be given to the percentage of fluoride content in drinking water in the patient's region and any additional sources of fluoride the patient is receiving, such as toothpaste and mouth rinse. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Early childhood intake of fluoride supplements and fluoride levels greater than 0.7 ppm in drinking water are associated with the risk of mild to severe dental fluorosis with associated aesthetic concerns. [16] [42] Ingesting greater than pea-sized amounts of toothpaste can lead to mild fluorosis in children younger than 6. [43] Children under 4 years of age are at risk of dental fluorosis in the permanent incisors and first molars since these teeth's calcification and maturation occur during these ages. Permanent premolars and second molars calcify and mature during 4 to 6 years of age; therefore, they are at risk of dental fluorosis. After the age of 6, the risk of dental fluorosis is less significant. [6] To avoid the risk of fluorosis in children and still reap the caries-preventive benefits of fluoride for all age groups, fluoride-containing products must be used appropriately. retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Since the implementation of water fluoridation, there has been a decrease in the prevalence of dental caries. The risk of dental fluorosis is mainly related to the systemic consumption of fluorides during the first 6 years of age. [6] However, early exposure to fluoride toothpaste has also been linked to dental fluorosis due to unintended swallowing. This risk should be considered when selecting the concentration of fluoride toothpaste for young children. [7] Fluoride-containing products, including toothpaste, rinse solutions, gels, foams, and varnishes, are indicated according to age and risk of caries.

Sodium monofluorophosphate is also the active ingredient in low‑dose fluoride toothpastes (0.0076 MG/MG, 0.008 MG/MG, and 0.0088 MG/MG) used for dental caries prevention. The Standardized MedDRA Query “الاختلال الوظيفي غير المحدد في الجهاز الهضمي” groups functional gastrointestinal disturbances—including الِتهابُ المَريءِ الارتجاعي, جَيّار, عُسْرُ الهَضْم, and مرض الارتداد المَعِدِيّ المَريئِيّ—facilitating systematic identification and management of unspecified GI disorders. retrieved_docs.content: it is listed among “other mineral supplements” in the ATC system. Fluoride compounds constitute the caries‑prophylactic agents within the ATC class of stomatological preparations, which also includes corticosteroids for local oral treatment and other local oral agents. Members of this group are tin fluoride, sodium fluoride, sodium monofluorophosphate and the commercial product Olaflur. Sodium fluoride appears in fluoride supplements, in combination products with calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate and magnesium hydroxide, and as the radioactive isotope NaF F‑18 used for diagnostic imaging. Sodium monofluorophosphate is the active ingredient in several dental pastes—Trade Joe Anticavity Peppermint (0.76 µg/100 µg), Aquafresh Kids Cavity Protection (1.10 mg/1 g), McKesson Fluoride (2.43 mg/1 g)—and in toothpaste formulations specified as 0.0076 mg/mg, 0.008 mg/mg and 0.0088 mg/mg sodium monofluorophosphate. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 124
retrieved_docs.content: Caries prophylactic agents are comprised of several fluoride‑based compounds, including fluorid cínatý, fluorid sodný (sodium fluoride), sodium monofluorophosphate and the commercial agent Olaflur, all organized under the broader preparation category S [Preparations] which lists fluorid cínatý, fluorid sodný and sodium monofluorophosphate as parent compounds. Fluorid sodný exists in a radiolabeled form (sodium fluoride‑18) and is also derived from a combination product containing calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide and sodium fluoride. Fluoride supplements are classified both within the fluorid sodný group and the sodium monofluorophosphate group, indicating that supplement formulations may contain either or both fluoride sources and are associated with fluoride (fluoridy) for dental or systemic health. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 363
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride supplements, selenium supplements, and zinc supplements are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) category of “other mineral supplements,” indicating that they are used to correct or prevent deficiencies of essential trace elements. Fluoride supplements are specifically linked to fluoridy, reflecting their role in providing fluoride for dental health and bone metabolism. Zinc supplements are further identified as members of zinc gluconate, a particular zinc salt formulation commonly employed to improve zinc bioavailability. Recognizing that these three agents belong to the same ATC subclass helps clinicians consider them together when addressing mineral deficiencies, while also acknowledging the distinct therapeutic actions of fluoride (fluoridy) and the specific formulation of zinc gluconate within zinc supplementation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1969
retrieved_docs.content: The oral tablet labeled “folic acid 1 MG / vitamin B12 0.5 MG” contains the two active ingredients vitamin B12 and folic acid, providing a combined supplement of these essential nutrients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2156
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride supplements and selenium supplement are both categorized as “other mineral supplements” within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification system. Fluoride supplements are specifically linked to fluoridy, indicating their role in providing fluoride. By sharing the same ATC subclass, these mineral supplements are recognized as related therapeutic agents used to address mineral deficiencies or support health through supplementation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 722
retrieved_docs.content: The combination of calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, and sodium fluoride is derived from sodium fluoride (fluorid sodný), indicating that this multi‑ingredient preparation is mapped from the basic sodium fluoride compound. Fluoride supplements are classified as members of the fluorid sodný group, meaning they are formulations that contain sodium fluoride as their active ingredient. Sodium fluoride (fluorid sodný) also exists in a specific isotopic form known as sodium fluoride F‑18, which is used in diagnostic imaging. In addition, fluoride supplements have a relationship with fluoridy, signifying that they provide fluoride ions to the body. Together, these connections illustrate how calcium and magnesium salts combined with sodium fluoride form the basis of fluoride supplementation, how such supplements belong to the broader sodium fluoride category, and how sodium fluoride can be presented in the F‑18 isotopic form for clinical applications.
